In the other cases (8/12) we find an upper limit to N^z_H (Table tab_lett2 ) ranging from 0.03 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 to 0.7 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 .

In 4 galaxies we obtained a detection of local X-ray absorption. In all cases our estimates of the column density are in close agreement with previously published measurements. See Chiaberge et al. (2002) and Donato et al. (2004) for 3C 270; Harris et al. (2002) and Donato et al. (2004) for 3C272.1; Hardcastle et al. (2005) for 3C 296 It is particularly instructive the inspection of their HST images (see Fig. hst ) as they unveil a connection between optical and X-ray absorption. The three sources for which we found the largest X-ray absorption show circumnuclear dusty disk structures, on scales between 300 pc - 1 kpc. All disks are seen at large inclinations, the less inclined being associated to 3C 270 whose axis form with the line of sight an angle 64^ jaffe96 . The fourth X-ray absorbed galaxy is 3C 272.1: its HST image show the presence of a low optical depth ( A_V 0.54 , bower97 ) filamentary dust lane, directly projected onto the nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3C 272.1 with NGC 4374/M 84, describing it as a Virgo elliptical with optical and infrared dust lane plus double-sided FR I jet and core.

quotes.quote: 3C 272.1 (NGC 4374, M 84); z=0.003536 A large well-studied elliptical in the Virgo cluster with a prominent dust lane visible in the optical, and faintly detectable in the NICMOS image bower+00 . The radio source is a double-sided FR I jet and core. However, its radio power is somewhat lower than the fiducial cutoff for radio-loud quasars. It is described as a ``weak radio galaxy'' by laingbridle87 .

sources.summary: The paper fits M84’s surface-brightness profile, finds a large outer-component luminosity fraction, and reports no significant tidal substructure beyond its smooth elliptical isophotes.

quotes.quote: For M84, we again find good agreement between those studies and ours, save for discrepancies in the position angle near ^ 1/4 4 . In this region, however, the ellipticity is so close to zero that the exact value of the position angle has little meaning. The S ersic and 2dV fits for M84 (given in Table sbfits ) yield a total luminosity of 7.2 and 6.5 10^ 10 L_ respectively, and in the 2dV fit the outer component carries 77 of the total luminosity.
M86 shows a number of small, relatively high surface streams, while M84 shows no evidence for significant substructure beyond its smooth elliptical isophotes.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes giant elliptical NGC 4374 with RGGR, using Sersic surface-brightness parameters for its stellar mass model and contrasting RGGR/MOND fits to its data.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374 NGC 4374 is a giant elliptical recently analyzed within RGGR . Here we present a variation of the analysis presented in that reference, namely we here do not directly use the photometric data with the S ersic extension to model the stellar mass, instead we here only use the S ersic model parameters that best fit the surface brightness of this galaxy. See Table N4374resultsTable and Fig. N4374CosmoSul . This is relevant to display that our results presented in are sufficiently robust to small changes on the baryonic mass content. htdp thbp In Ref. it is shown hat MOND fits better the NGC 4374 observational data than Newtonian gravity without dark matter.

sources.summary: NGC 4374 is described as a Virgo round elliptical with a rapidly rotating nuclear gas disk, declining inner dispersion, and only mild outer rotation.

quotes.quote: This Virgo round elliptical (E1) galaxy harbors a rapidly rotating nuclear gas disk Bower:1998a . Napo:2011a used bright PNe as tracers to measure the stellar kinematics out to 350 arcsec. They found a steeply declining dispersion profile in the inner 30 arcsec beyond which the profile flattens out at a value of 230 km s ^ 1 . As might be expected for such a slow rotator Emsellem:2011br , we detect only mild to no rotation in the outer parts of the combined kinematic map, in agreement with Coccato:2009je .

sources.summary: The paper gives K-band decomposition notes for NGC4374, describing its morphology, fitted components, residual structures, and why the adopted model is preferred over simpler alternatives.

quotes.quote: NGC4374 (M84, Fig. fig:NGC4374 ) is a giant elliptical galaxy with a large core. The ellipticity is low ( 0.2 ) at small radii and decreases to almost zero outside 100 . The isophotes are boxy throughout.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4374 as a core early-type galaxy with a post-star-forming MIR nucleus, anomalous PAHs, fine structure, and evidence that late accretion triggered star formation.

quotes.quote: The MIR spectra indicate nuclei with different kinds of activity from AGN (class-4, M87 and NGC 4261) to post-star forming (MIR class-2 with anomalous PAHs, NGC 4374, NGC 4636, NGC 4552) to the quiescent nucleus of NGC 4365 (class-0). Both SR, the majority, and FR show signatures of kinematical and morphological peculiarities suggesting the action of accretion episodes. Of particular interest is NGC 4382, which although showing strong shell features (see e.g. figure 9 in Kormendy09 ) has a nearly passively evolving nucleus (class-1). This is not the case for NGC 4374 and NGC 4552, that both show fine structure see Sansom00 , although less prominent than that in NGC 4382. The class-2 spectrum of NGC 4552 reveals the presence of gas and the additional presence of anomalous PAHs, as in NGC 4374, points to the occurrence of a recent star formation event.
AGN feedback does not prevent a late star formation episode in the bright Es NGC 4374, NGC 4636 and NGC 4552.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4374 has variable Chandra normalization and large soft/hard flux changes, while cross-instrument comparison is blocked by annular contamination.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374 : SMF1 is used for the simultaneous fit with Chandra data, with variations of Norm_2 (73 ) in a period of five years. Flux variation of 64 (71 ) in the soft (hard) band during the same period are found. Data from different instruments are not compared because the annular region contributes 84 in Chandra data. Short term variations in the soft and total bands are found from one Chandra observation below 2 confidence level.

sources.summary: For 3C 272.1, the UV data favor a YSP, while low redshift complicates photometric scaling and MIR/FIR data fit cool and hot dust.

quotes.quote: 3C 272.1. The FUV and NUV data suggest that a YSP is present and not a power-law component. The high ^2 value is due to the 2MASS data being systematically higher than the model SED (see Fig. fig1 ). We note that 3C 272.1 is at very low redshift (z=0.0031), which may have contributed to the difficulty of correctly scaling all photometric bands. MIR and FIR data are available, which can be fitted by a mix of dust with temperatures of 35 K ( M_d = 6.0 10^3 M_ ) and 350 K ( M_d = 0.5 M_ ).

sources.summary: The paper measures M84 in the Virgo elliptical nova survey, finding 19 novae, an annual nova rate of 95, and a luminosity-specific rate of 3.0 per 10^10 K-band solar luminosities.

quotes.quote: To explore this idea, relative nova rates in three Virgo elliptical galaxies, M87, M49 and M84, which have significantly different GC specific frequencies ( S_ N ) of 14, 3.6, and 1.6, respectively, were measured over the course of 4 epochs spanning a period of 14 months. To simplify the analysis, observations of the nearly equidistant galaxies were made on the same nights, with the same integration times, and through the same filter (H ), so that the relative numbers of novae discovered would reflect the relative nova rates. At the conclusion of our survey we found a total of 27 novae associated with M87, 37 with M49, and 19 with M84. After correcting for survey completeness, we found annual nova rates of 154^ +23 _ 19 , 189^ +26 _ 22 , and 95^ +15 _ 14 , for M87, M49, and M84, respectively, corresponding to K band luminosity-specific nova rates of 3.8 1.0 , 3.4 0.6 , and 3.0 0.6 novae per year per 10^ 10 L_ K, .

sources.summary: M 84 is discussed as an elliptical galaxy whose RGS spectrum is dominated by Fe XVII lines and shows O VII excess, especially in the resonant line.

quotes.quote: Fig:Spectra_ratios we show the RGS spectra of three interesting sources, the Centaurus cluster, M 84 and M 89 (from top to bottom).

A similar trend is observed in Fornax, M 49, M 84, NGC 4636--5044--5813, and Perseus. The M 84 and M 89 elliptical galaxies, whose spectra are dominated by the Fe XVII lines, show an O VII excess with respect to the two-phase cie model. Interestingly, in M 84 (and NGC 5846) the O VII resonant line at 21.6 is in excess, while in M 89 (and NGC 4636) the excess is shown by the forbidden line at 22.1 .

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 4374 kinematics, describing its rotation, velocity dispersion, halo behavior, and relevant photometric or interaction features in the early-type-galaxy survey.

quotes.quote: Also known as M84, it is a bright E1 galaxy in the Virgo cluster. The galaxy does not show significant rotation in the innermost 0.4 , as reported by 2004MNRAS.352..721E and , while the velocity dispersion steeply decreases from 310 at the center to 200 at 1 . The PN velocity field shows evidence for rotation only at large radii ( R 2 ), with constant amplitude 60 and along a direction compatible with the minor axis. The velocity dispersion is rather constant around 220 up to 6 .

sources.summary: The paper uses an outer-region template to model NGC 4374, improves the nuclear fit with a very broad H component, but flags constraints as difficult.

quotes.quote: For the HST /STIS spectra of two galaxies (namely NGC 4374 and NGC 4552) the [ S II ] lines were so blended and the [ O I ] lines almost absent, that it was challenging to perform the modeling using them as reference (Fig. Panel_NGC4374 and Panel_NGC4552 ). Thus we decided to fit the spectrum of a region situated a few pixels away from the nucleus and use this result to model the narrow component of the nuclear region. This was motivated by the assumption that the narrow component is tracing the kinematics of the disc of the galaxy, thus it should also be visible in regions outside the very center of the system.
According to our modeling, the fit is improved in the three galaxies by adding a very broad H component to the H [ N II ] complex.

sources.summary: The paper pays special attention to NGC 4374 (M84) because its central Chandra data are high quality; it hosts an FR-I radio source and remains above the precipitation zone.

quotes.quote: In this work, we pay particular attention to NGC 4374 (M84), NGC 1316 (Fornax A), and IC 4296 because Chandra observations of the central 10 kpc of those galaxies have the best signal-to-noise ratios among those in our sample, and we are most interested in atmospheric properties closest to the center. Each galaxy represents a different manifestation of a powerful radio source. M84 hosts a Fanaroff-Riley-I radio source Abbreviated as FR-I and defined as a radio source in which the low brightness regions of the radio source are further from the galaxy than the high brightness regions Fanaroff1974 Harris2002_M84 .
In our sample, the t_ cool /t_ ff profile for NGC 4374 remains above the precipitation zone, and NGC 1316 is consistent with the multiphase galaxy pattern from Voit2015b .

sources.summary: M 84 is described as a central Virgo radio galaxy with deflected lobes or tails, X-ray cavities, and ICM interaction visible in LOFAR data.

quotes.quote: VCC 763 (NGC 4374, M 84) The radio galaxy M 84 is the second-brightest radio source in the central Virgo cluster after M 87 and a well-studied example for AGN-feedback in non-central galaxies Finoguenov2001ITALChandra/ITALCluster,Birzan2004AGalaxies,Finoguenov2008,Laing2011DeepLobes,Bambic2023AGNRadius . The radio lobes/tails are deflected after 5 kpc, a clear sign of interaction with the ICM. They coincide with Chandra-detected X-ray cavities Finoguenov2001ITALChandra/ITALCluster,Finoguenov2008,Bambic2023AGNRadius . In the 144 MHz LOFAR images, the source is not significantly more extended than at GHz frequencies.

sources.summary: M84 is presented as an early-type galaxy whose Chandra H-shaped X-ray structure is produced by radio lobes displacing hot gas without strong shock heating.

quotes.quote: For example, in the E1 galaxy M84 (NGC4374), Chandra observed an unusual X-ray morphology which is explained by the effect of the radio lobes on the hot gas . The X-ray emission appears H shaped, with a bar extending east-west with two nearly parallel filaments perpendicular to this bar. The complex X-ray surface brightness distribution arises from the presence of two radio lobes (approximately north and south of the galaxy) that produce two low density regions surrounded by higher density X-ray filaments. As with Perseus/NGC1275 and Hydra A, the filaments, defining the H shaped emission, have gas temperatures comparable to the gas in the central and outer regions of the galaxy and hence argue against any strong shock heating of the galaxy atmosphere by the radio plasma.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4374 as hosting multiple nuclear point sources, knotty diffuse emission, radio jet-like structures, and a radio-loud AGN dominating the hard X-rays.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374 (L2, E1) has four point sources in its nucleus. The brightest one is at the center and has a hard X-ray spectrum. There is also significant soft, knotty, diffuse emission, although the knots could be embedded point sources. There are many models that give good fits, in particular a power-law and a plasma model plus a power-law.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4374 as the E1 radio galaxy Messier 84 in Virgo and finds dark-halo fits favoring F_d about 8 and large halo radii.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374 This E1 radio galaxy (Messier 84) is a member of the Virgo cluster. Fits to this galaxy favour the presence of a dark halo, with F_ d 8 and R 100 to 1000 R_ e . Unusually, for the second axis, the F_ d =9 case improves on the basic ``stars only'' model (although F_ d 8 is still the best fit).

sources.summary: NGC 4374 is the elliptical test case; the paper compares its stellar photometry and velocity-dispersion contributions from stars, a black hole, and NFW dark matter.

quotes.quote: Real data: NGC4374 Starting from these preliminary considerations, we can move to the analysis of a real system: NGC 4374 (see Table tab:tabdata ), which is our test case for elliptical galaxy dynamics.

For NGC 4374 there are different literature models of its stellar photometry (mainly depending on the extension of the adopted datasets): a typical De Vaucouleurs profile (a Sersic profile with index m=4 ), with R_ eff = 5.97 kpc Cappellari06 ; a Sersic profile with R_ eff =11.69 kpc and m=7.98 Kormendy09 ; and a Sersic profile with R_ eff = 9.34 kpc and m=6.11 (N+11).

Looking to the left panel of Fig. fig:NGC4374 we see how the stellar component dominates the velocity dispersion profile only in the very central regions ( R 1.0 kpc), with the black hole contribution being important only for very small scales, and the NFW dark matter becoming dominant in the line-of-sight velocity dispersion profile at 10 kpc, which corresponds to 1-2 R_ eff .

sources.summary: The atlas gives NGC 4374's AGN or host classification, redshift context, and object-specific MIR or multiwavelength observational properties.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374 is a dusty giant elliptical in the Virgo cluster at a distance of D= 17.1 2.4 Mpc (NED redshift-independent median) with FR I radio morphology and an radio-loud AGN classified either as LINER ho_search_1997-1 or Sy 2 veron-cetty_catalogue_2010 . Two parallel dust lanes are seen perpendicular to the radio jet axis with one crossing the nucleus (PA 60 ; hansen_properties_1985,jaffe_hubble_1994,bower_nuclear_1997 ). The first N band photometry of NGC 4374 was obtained with IRTF in 1983 impey_infrared_1986 where the nucleus was weakly detected at 21 mJy (5.7 aperture).

sources.summary: The paper records NGC 4374's adopted photometric profile or decomposition, giving bulge/scale parameters and comparing those choices with alternative literature fits for the structural analysis.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374: The galaxy is a giant core elliptical Rusli2013b . We measure R_e=84.0'' , while Vika2012 derive implausibly small bulge radii from one-component ( R_e=26.8'' ) or bulge plus disk ( R_e=7.2'' ) fits. The M/L is scaled to the distance of KormendyHo2013 and from I to V- band using the (V-I) Hyperleda color.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4374, Messier 84, as a Virgo giant elliptical with collimated jets, diffuse lobes, extended pear-like radio emission, and cavities.

quotes.quote: NGC 4374 Messier 84 or NGC 4374 is a giant elliptical galaxy in the Virgo A group, which is a part of the Virgo cluster together with NGC 4406 (Section sec:ngc4406 ), NGC 4486 (Section sec:ngc4486 ), and NGC 4552 (Section sec:ngc4552 ). \ The radio emission in the archival B configuration data (Figure fig:chandra_vla_b g) shows well-defined and in the central regions well-collimated jets terminating in diffuse plum-like lobes first published by laing1987 with the northern lobe brighter than the southern and bent to the east. VLA D configuration data reveal pear-like diffuse emission extending beyond the B configuration data.

sources.summary: NGC4374 is treated as an off-axis BeppoSAX source, detected only at low significance and converted to a 2-10 keV luminosity under a power-law assumption.

quotes.quote: NGC4374 This source was observed at 15 arcmin off-axis. It is detected at only the 3.2 level having 71 22 counts. Assuming a power-law spectrum with a =1.9 slope, we obtain a 2-10 keV flux of 3.4 10^ 13 , corresponding to a luminosity of 1.1 10^ 40 .

sources.summary: The paper cites NGC 4374/M84 as a warped-disk case where models explain filamentary dust morphology and infer a 68 degree viewing angle to the disk symmetry axis.

quotes.quote: Models of a warped disk have been used to explain the filamentary dust morphology in NGC 4374 (M84; Quillen Bower ) and both the morphology and kinematics of the interstellar matter in NGC 5128 (Quillen ). For NGC 4374 and NGC 5128 the studies infer an angle between the line of sight and the symmetry axis of the warped disk of 68 and 60 respectively.
Such a model was explored in detail for NGC 4374 by Quillen Bower ( ).

gmw_00000210.name: NGC 4388
gmw_00000210.overview: NGC 4388 is a highly inclined Virgo Cluster spiral galaxy [arXiv:0712.0671, arXiv:astro-ph/0701610] classified as a Seyfert 2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0304136, arXiv:2005.08569]. The galaxy is undergoing heavy ram-pressure stripping [arXiv:0712.0671, arXiv:1308.5760], which has truncated its neutral gas disk [arXiv:1001.3597] and created an extensive, 120-kiloparsec-long atomic hydrogen tail [arXiv:0712.0671, arXiv:1806.05887].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4388 as a ram-pressure-stripped Virgo spiral with long ionised and HI tails, high cluster-relative velocity, and stripping broadly consistent with the simulations.

quotes.quote: The Virgo spiral galaxy NGC 4388 seems to be an excellent example of a ram pressure stripped galaxy. It is known to have a 35 long tail of ionised gas ( yoshida02,yoshida04 ). Additionally, oosterloo05 have found a 120 long HI tail associated with this galaxy. NGC 4388 has a high radial velocity of 1400 (see vollmer03a and references therein) with respect to the cluster mean.

A reasonable value for the local ICM density at the position of NGC 4388 is a few 10^ 28 . With a likely velocity of about 2000 , it experiences a ram pressure of about 10^ 11 . According to our simulations, this is enough to be stripped heavily, so regarding the degree of stripping, simulations and observations agree. Regarding the combination of orbital velocity and local ICM density, in our simulations, there is no case directly comparable to NGC 4388.

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4388 as a Virgo spiral whose outer disk star formation was halted by ram-pressure stripping about 190 Myr ago.

quotes.quote: The goal of our study is to recover the stripping age of the Virgo spiral galaxy NGC 4388, i.e. the time elapsed since the halt of star formation in the outer galactic disk using a combined analysis of optical spectra and photometry. We performed VLT FORS2 long-slit spectroscopy of the inner star-forming and outer gas-free disk of NGC 4388. We developed a non-parametric inversion tool that allows us to reconstruct the star formation history of a galaxy from spectroscopy and photometry.

The results from the non-parametric inversion were refined by applying a parametric inversion method. The star formation history of the unperturbed galactic disk is flat. The non-parametric method yields a rapid decline of star formation 200 Myr ago in the outer disk. The parametric method is not able to distinguish between an instantaneous and a long-lasting star formation truncation. The time since the star formation has dropped by a factor of two from its pre-stripping value is 190 30 Myr.

NGC 4388 is a highly inclined Seyfert 2 spiral galaxy (of type Sab) with m_B=12.2 mag and a radial velocity of V_ rad 1400 km s ^ 1 with respect to the cluster mean. It is located at a projected distance of 1.3 ^ ( 400 kpc at 16.7 Mpc distance) from the Virgo cluster center (M87). NGC 4388 has lost 85 of its HI gas mass ( cay ). The HI distribution is strongly truncated within the optical disk.

sources.summary: NGC 4388 is described as an edge-on Seyfert spiral with a hidden broad-line region, high-ionization mid-IR lines, hard X-ray AGN emission, a radio jet, and detected CO.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 NGC 4388 is a nearby edge-on spiral galaxy located at 18.2 Mpc. A weak broad H component has been reported by Shields1996 and it is classified as Seyfert 1.9 by Ho1997 . However Ruiz1994 did not find evidence for broad components in the near-IR Pa line. A hidden broad line region (HBLR) is detected in optical polarized light Young1996 . Both the mid-IR [ Ne 5 ] and [ O 4 ] high-ionization emission lines are detected in the IRS spectrum of this galaxy Tommasin2010 .

The absorption corrected hard X-ray luminosity of the AGN is 3.0 10 ^ 41 erg s ^ 1 Cappi2006 . A radio jet is observed in this galaxy Falcke1998 . We detect six CO lines from J_ up =4 to 9 in the SLW \ spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4388 as a Virgo spiral undergoing ram-pressure stripping, arguing that its long gas tail was created mostly by interaction with the Virgo intracluster medium.

quotes.quote: One of the best candidates for the observational RPS study is a nearby spiral galaxy NGC 4388 (N4388 hereafter). It is located at a projected distance of 1.3 degree away from the core of the Virgo cluster (M87), and its radial velocity of 2525 km s ^ 1 is about by 1500 km s ^ 1 higher than that of M87. Such a high velocity is thought to have enhanced the RPS in this object. In fact, as reported in, e.g., 1989ApJ...344..171K and 1990AJ....100..604C , N4388 is one of the most deficient spiral galaxies in the Virgo cluster. Recently, more intriguing RPS features have been unveiled: 2002ApJ...567..118Y discovered in \ a plume of ionized gas extending 35 kpc to the northeast.

Hence, our current result suggests that the N4388-M86 interaction is insufficient for the RPS, and the \ tail was created mostly by colliding with the Virgo ICM. Another interesting aspect is to understand phase changes of the materials removed from N4388. Given the associated hot plasma mass and \ mass of 4.5 0.8 10^ 8 ^ 0.5 M_ ( 3.2) and 3.4 10^ 8 M_ (OG05), respectively, the ionized-to-atomic gas mass ratio in the tail is therefore 1 , which is an order of magnitude higher than the typical values within disk galaxies (e.g., 2009ApJ...697...79R ; 2012ApJ...756..183B ).

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 4388 has well-ordered central rotation, a misaligned 100 pc nuclear disk, cold molecular gas kinematics, and possible AGN-feeding structure near its megamaser disk.

quotes.quote: However, in NGC 4388 we have excellent S/N, and uncover a variety of interesting features: The stellar velocity field demonstrates well-ordered rotation aligned with the kpc-scale disk. Although there is net rotation in the stars, still they are kinematically hot, with V/ 0.6 , likely dominated by the dispersion in the kpc-scale bulge. In the inner 1 \ (100 pc), we also see evidence for a nuclear disk, offet in PA by 15 \ from the kpc-scale disk. The evidence includes disky isophotes in the /WFC3 F160W image and a distinct drop in stellar velocity dispersion on one side of the putative disk (the other side apparently extincted).

The \ gas also shows regular kinematics on > 100 pc scales, with a well-defined rotation field that is aligned with the kpc-scale disk. With a higher rotation amplitude, the gas is considerably colder than the stars ( V/ 1.5 ). We also see a kink in the rotation curve on 100 pc scales. Intriguingly, the \ rotation within 100 pc appears to align with the megamaser disk.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC4388 as a high-inclination spiral with tightly wound arms forming a ring, central edge-on nuclear disk, dust lanes, and extended disk/envelope components.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 NGC4388 (Figures fig:NGC4388-1 and fig:NGC4388-2 ) is a spiral galaxy seen at high inclination (disk q=0.35 i 70 ). The tightly-wound spiral arms are visually defined between SMA 30 and 50 ( 2.8 and 4.6 ). They form a ring that can be identified in the surface brightness and ellipticity profiles. In the center of NGC4388 there is a bright, central ( 2.5" / 200 ) disk, seen almost edge-on with very low axis ratio, and at a 15 misalignment from the large-scale major axis. The entire region interior to the ring contains several dust lanes.

We identify the inner exponential as the main disk, while the outer exponential ``envelope'' as a very extended disk, due to its high flattening ( q 0.1 ) and 100" ( 9 ) scale radius. In addition to the bulge, disk and nucleus (point source), we account for the nuclear disk, spiral arms, and envelope with exponential profiles. The spiral arms are modeled as a ring with an inner truncation.

sources.summary: The paper finds strong circular polarization in the core and northern jet of NGC 4388, with weak linear polarization and steep spectra supporting Faraday conversion.

quotes.quote: For NGC 4388, the conversion falls into the category, p>2 (Eqn. convp>2practical ). Although we do not have information on all of the physical parameters that enter into a CP calculation, we can ask what reasonable values might fit the observations. For example, at L-band, no linear polarization is measured at the core, precluding an estimate of a lower limit for U_o . In principle, we could make an estimate of the amount of L-band linear polarization that is to be Faraday converted by observing the linear polarization at C-band along with its spectral index, and then extrapolating to L-band. Unfortunately, the C-band linear polarization at the nucleus is also very weak, likely due to significant Faraday depolarization; with a degree of polarization at the nucleus of only 0.2 in C-band dam16 and a peak C-band value of 9.0 mJy beam ^ 1 (for the same spatial resolution), this corresponds to only 18 Jy beam ^ 1 at C-band -- much less than the observed CP at L-band.

CP is observed in the jet of NGC 4388 also and is remarkably strong, even stronger than at the core ( m_C = 3.4 , Table table:detections ). We have measured jet values at the position of the peak V intensity in the jet, marked with a blue `plus' in Fig. fig:allgalaxies . We note, however that, again, the band-to-band spectral index is steep, as expected for Faraday conversion and spectral indices where measurable (Table table:spectral_indices ), are similar to values in the core.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as a Virgo Seyfert 2 where RM-synthesis reveals new halo and disk features, nuclear outflows, polarization filaments, and wind-speed requirements.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 In the case of this Virgo galaxy, the sensitivity provided by the use of the RM-synthesis technique on our C band-C configuration data VII was key to revealing a large number of new faint features in the halo (Figure fig:n4388 ). With an exceptionally low rms noise of 2.3 Jy/beam and a resolution of 5.33 arcsec in polarized intensity, we are also able to resolve some interesting features of the disk never seen before. h  This is a Seyfert 2 galaxy with many processes occurring at the same time. There are several outflow events detected at different wavelengths (i.e., H , X-ray, HI) whose kinematics and morphology are strongly modified by the strong ram-pressure action of the intracluster medium (ICM). The brightest emission in radio polarization in our VLA map is detected at the northern part of the nuclear outflow, where the magnetic field vectors are oriented along the outflow structure.

Our data also show for the first time a well defined southern nuclear outflow where the magnetic field vectors are distributed in an S-shaped structure, which is an indication of a connection between the bright radio spots detected above the disk and the nucleus of the galaxy. The halo also shows two polarization filaments to the north and south of the nuclear outflow that extend up to 5  kpc in the case of the northern filament. These structures seem to also be connected to the nuclear activity due to its symmetry with respect to the centre.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NICER spectra of the Seyfert-2 galaxy NGC 4388, finding strong but Compton-thin obscuration, variable column density, Fe K emission, and ionized absorption.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of the time-averaged spectrum of the Seyfert-2 active galaxy NGC 4388, obtained by NICER . The intrinsic strength of the reflection spectrum in NGC 4388, the large collecting area and favorable pass band of NICER , and a net exposure of 105.6 ks yielded an exceptionally sensitive spectrum. Using two independent families of models, the intrinsic spectrum from the central engine is found to be highly obscured but not Compton-thick.

A more recent measurement with NuSTAR (in late 2013) recorded a column density about twice as large; the robustness of this variability is reinforced by the use of consistent models and procedures. The neutral Fe K line in the NICER spectrum is nominally resolved and consistent with an origin in the optical broad line region (BLR). The data also require ionized absorption in the Fe K band, similar to the ``warm absorbers'' detected in Seyfert-1 active galactic nuclei (AGN).
NGC 4388 is an Sb galaxy in the Virgo Cluster. Recent work has estimated its distance at d=18.0 Mpc (Sorce et al. \ 2014).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC4388 as an edge-on Sy2 with ionisation cones, prior cross-like radio morphology, and new NE-SW double emission with a flat northern nucleus and steeper southern component.

quotes.quote: NGC4388 It is a Sy2 edge-on galaxy in the Virgo cluster, and it is known for having wide ionisation cones extending well above and below the stellar disk e.g. AyaniIye1989 . VLA observations reveal a cross-like morphology extending for in the N direction 19 arcsec (P.A. 10 deg), with a central double of 1.9 arcsec, constituted by a southern component which may be associated to a spike in optical emission lines e.g. HummelSaikia1991,FalckeWilsonSimpson1998,HU01,DamasSegovia2016 and a northern component forming a collimated jet e.g. Kukula1999,Mundell2000 . The nucleus is detected by GirolettiPanessa2009 with EVN only at 1.6 GHz, with a T_B 10 ^6 K, which can not rule out a thermal origin. Smith2016 studied it with VLA in C configuration at 22 GHz ( 1 arcsec resolution), finding an elongated morphology, but no double component, with flux density 3.26 . Behar2018 observed it with CARMA at 100 GHz reporting flux density of 3.58 0.34 .

Our radio maps reveal a double morphology in the direction NE-SW, with a ridge of radio emission between the components which are 1.9 arcsec apart ( 330 pc). The spectral slope of the northern component (where the nucleus is believed to reside) is flat ( = 0.27 0.11) while the southern component exhibits a steeper spectrum ( = 0.87 0.09). The position quoted by Smith2016 is compatible with our northern component, and the flux density of our northern component is compatible with both their 22 GHz flux and the 100 GHz flux of Behar2018 , the same being valid for the high-frequency spectral slope.

sources.summary: The paper presents Chandra observations of Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC 4388, resolving kpc-scale extended hard X-ray and Fe K emission linked to circumnuclear gas and AGN reprocessing.

quotes.quote: We present Chandra imaging and spectral observations of Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC 4388. Three extended X-ray structures around the nucleus on kpc scale are well imaged, allowing an in-depth spatially resolved study. Both the extended hard continuum and the Fe K line show similar morphology, consistent with a scenario where the ionizing emission from nucleus is reprocessed by circumnuclear cold gas, resulting in a weak reflection continuum and an associated neutral Fe K line. This has been seen in other Compton thick active galactic nuclei (AGN), but NGC 4388 is one of the rare cases with a lower column density ( N_ H < 1.25 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 ) along the line of sight. Significant differences in equivalent width of the Fe K emission line are found for the nuclear and extended regions, which could be ascribed to different column densities or scattering angles with respect to the line of sight, rather than variations in iron abundances.

NGC 4388 ( z = 0.00842 ) is one of the brightest Seyfert 2 galaxies at hard X-ray energies Baumgartner_2013 with log L _ 20-50 ^ int =43.23 erg s^ 1 2017Ricci . Several broadband observations of this source have been performed with X-ray missions such as ASCA iwasawa1997asca , INTEGRAL and Swift fedorova2011studying , XMM-Newton 2004Beckmann , BeppoSAX elvis2004unveiling , Suzaku shirai2008detailed , NuSTAR 2017ApJ...843...89K supporting the hypothesis of a powerful hidden nucleus. Based on more than 30 years of X-ray observations reported in the literature, the column density in this source shows variability spanning from 2 to 7 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 which are tabulated in Table 2.

sources.summary: HRS 144 is a radio-slope outlier with Milky-Way-like SFR; the paper attributes its shallow slope to a Seyfert nucleus and H i absorption, making its radio continuum non-purely star-forming.

quotes.quote: We clearly see that two galaxies (HRS 25 and 144) are outliers in the left panel of Fig. fig:comparehist , while they do not show any deviation from the bulk of the population when the shorter frequency data are included.

However, HRS 25 and 144 have a SFR similar to the Milky Way Boselli2015 and a critical frequency between MWA frequencies and 1500 [ MHz ] . This would be difficult to explain by the conjecture of Schober2017 . Possible reasons for this are 1) poor statistics, i.e., fewer data can constrain the fitted slope only weakly, and 2) physical peculiarity of the galaxy.

In contrast, HRS 144 is identified as a Seyfert galaxy from the BPT diagram e.g., Baldwin1981, Kewley2001, Kauffmann2003, Schawinski2007 , and the galactic nuclei affects the spectral slope. Further, an H i absorption towards the core of HRC 144 is reported from the VIVA observation 2009AJ....138.1741C . Hence, it would be natural that the radio continuum emission is not consistent with that of a purely star-forming galaxy. In this case, the shallow slope is physical.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4388 with prominent extended soft emission, finds the simple models under tension but mutually consistent in N_H, and classifies it as N_H variable.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 Data reduction/fitting: observations with Obs. ID 9276, 9277 and 2983 were not considered because they used HETG/LETG grating. This galaxy has a prominent extended emission, likely the result of star formation. We used a 12 radius region (background annulus at radii 20 30 ) to include it all in the analysis of data. The brightness and closeness of this galaxy results in great data quality, and therefore more substructure is appreciated in the soft emission. We used a two- apec model to describe it.

Analysis of results: The best-fit of all models to the data shows significant tension ( T 20 ). This may be a result of the large number of counts available for this source, compared to that of the rest of the sample. It may be that our model is too simple to adequately fit it. However, no obvious problem is seen in the fit residuals that may point toward any specific issues. This source is likely a good candidate to implement a more complex treatment of the reflection component, such as the scenarios mentioned in Sect. Discussion . Despite the poorer fit, the models show remarkable agreement, particularly in the N_ H,los determinations. The largest discrepancy is in the photon index obtained by , which is largely incompatible with those of and . The values of _ obs obtained via and are also incompatible, with favoring an edge-on scenario, while suggests a much more inclined viewing angle. Our results are mostly in agreement with those of Zhao2020 , although they obtain much higher N_ H,av , on the order of 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 . Even if the fit to the data might be improved by using more complex models, it is clear that allowing both intrinsic flux and N_ H,los variability significantly improves the fit. Taking this into account, as well as the derived p-values, we classify this source as ` N_ H,los variable'.

sources.summary: Paper presents VLA FRAMEx observations of radio-quiet AGN NGC 4388, finding polarized 4-12 GHz structure, a broken-power-law component, and radio continuum likely driven by AGN winds.

quotes.quote: In this work, we introduce our calibration and imaging methods for this survey, and we present our results and analysis for the radio quiet AGN NGC 4388. We calibrated and imaged these data using the multi-term, multi-frequency synthesis imaging algorithm to determine its spatial, spectral and polarization structure across a continuous 4-12 GHz band. In the AGN, we measure a broken power law spectrum with =-0.06 below a break frequency of 7.3 GHz and =-0.34 above. We detect polarization at sub-arcsecond resolution across both the AGN and a secondary radio knot. We compare our results to ancillary data and find that the VLA radio continuum is likely due to AGN winds interacting with the local interstellar medium that gets resolved away at sub-parsec spatial scales as probed by the Very Long Baseline Array.

We suggest that Component B is not a jet but rather is consistent with a radiative outflow interacting with the ISM. AGN photoionization is likely to dominate at the radius of Component B, and is evidenced by multi-wavelength studies of the ionization cone. Component B shows a steep spectral index of =-1.07 0.11 and is potentially produced via enhanced magnetic fields from wind-shocked emission, which in turn produces the observed synchrotron radiation.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as a Virgo spiral with truncated neutral gas, ionized and X-ray plumes, and extended gas likely stripped by ram pressure from the M86 halo.

quotes.quote: A prime candidate for further study is the Virgo spiral NGC 4388. This galaxy is moving with a velocity of (at least) 1500 \ through the Virgo cluster and is one of its most \ deficient galaxies (having lost 85 of its ; cay90 ). Its \ disk is truncated to well within the optical disk cay90 . Moreover, the galaxy has a large plume of ionised gas, visible in H , extending 35 kpc off the galactic plane to the NE yos02 .

Here we present new \ observations of the region NE of NGC 4388. These observations show that the gas NE of NGC 4388 extends much further than previously thought. The data provide strong evidence that ram pressure pushed this gas from NGC 4388. Most likely it is not the ICM of the Virgo cluster itself, but the hot halo around M86 that is stripping NGC 4388.

sources.summary: The paper compares simulations with NGC 4388, interpreting its long Virgo H I tail as ram-pressure-stripped gas and arguing its slightly S-shaped morphology is not a von Karman street.

quotes.quote: The case of NGC 4388 So far, the only example of a long tail of cool ram pressure stripped gas is the case of NGC 4388 in the Virgo Cluster ( oosterloo05 ). This galaxy's tail has a length of 120 in the plane of the sky, the tail width flares from about 15 near the galaxy to about 40 at a projected distance of 100 behind the galaxy. The ridge of maximum flux density suggests a slight S-shape of the tail. In order to disentangle the true properties of the wake, the velocity component in the plane of the sky would have to be known. The length of the tail indicates that this component is substantial.

Consequently, a substantial velocity component in the plane of the sky as indicated by the long tail suggests that NGC 4388's true velocity with respect to the cluster mean is rather high. Thus, it is very likely that this galaxy is moving supersonically through the ICM. Therefore, our simulations suggest that the implied S-shape in the tail is not due to regular von-Karman oscillations.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as the exceptional Virgo spiral with a long H i tail, detected extraplanar gas, and a post-ram-pressure stage before tail evaporation.

quotes.quote: In the Virgo cluster an extended gas tail is only observed in one spiral galaxy. This exception is NGC 4388 which is located at a projected distance of 1.3 ^ o ( 0.4 Mpc We use a distance of 17 Mpc to the Virgo cluster ) from the Virgo cluster center (M87) and hosts a Seyfert 2 nucleus. Yoshida et al. (2002) discovered a very large H plume that extends up to 35 kpc north eastwards from the galactic disk which is seen almost edge-on. This region contains 10^ 5 M _ of ionized gas.

NGC 4388 We observed 6 5 positions centered on the H i plume of NGC 4388 (Oosterloo van Gorkom 2005) (Fig. fig:n4388ext ). The spectrum centered on NGC 4388 is labeled with a ``G''. The observing conditions did not permit us to obtain interference-free spectra of two positions in the north-east of NGC 4388. In the corresponding boxes we have replaced the spectra by a solid line. The sinusoidal behaviour of the spectra in the west and the east is most probably due to sidelobe detection of M87. We clearly detect H i line emission in 4 positions: in NGC 4388 and in 3 positions to the north west of the galactic disk. These positions are labeled with ``tail''. One of the spectra shows a double line profile consistent with the WSRT observations of Oosterloo van Gorkom (2005). Our deep H i observations show that there is no significant amount of atomic hydrogen beyond this H i tail.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as a highly inclined, complex spiral and a well-studied minor-axis outflow case with nuclear, spiral-arm, and large-scale outflow emission.

quotes.quote: The R band image of NGC 4388 (Figure f:ngc4388 ) 18 show a complicated morphology, where the spiral arms are prominently featured in this highly inclined galaxy. Also present are several circumnuclear dusty regions. The narrowband images, however, show a much more intriguing morphology.

NGC 4388 is one of the best studied cases of minor axis outflows e.g., pog88b,pog89,vei99a,vei99b,yos02,vei03 . Our map shows evidence for spiral arms with a number of H ii regions, as well as the nucleus whose morphology is reminiscent of a bar joining the base of the two spiral arms. Also visible in the high-contrast image shown in Figures f:fourha and f:ngc4388zoom (zoom-in) are the outflows extending 57 (4.6 kpc) to the N and 31 (2.5 kpc) to the S of the midplane of the galaxy. These are only small sections of the large-scale outflows which extend out to distances of 35 kpc from the nucleus yos02 . The map shows strong emission from the nucleus, which extends in the southern direction.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as a Virgo Seyfert with inclined disk, X-ray nucleus, radio outflow, extraplanar conical emission, and central high-dispersion gas components.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 The galaxy belongs to the Virgo cluster Phillips82,Yasuda . The galactic disc of PA 90 is inclined at i 78 VeilleuxBar4388 , with the north rim on the near side. The nucleus is a strong X-ray source e.g., Hanson90,Takano91,Lebrun92,Iwasawa97 . Radio-emission morphology suggests a collimated AGN-driven outflow reaching 200 pc south of the nucleus e.g., Stone,Falcke98 . Weak broad H emission implies that a hidden type-1 nucleus is present Filippenko .

Extended optical emission has two components of different excitation, one associated with the galactic disc and another reaching 50 (4 kpc) above the galactic plane in the form of two opposite cones e.g., Pogge4388,Corbin,Falcke98,Veilleux4388 .
We detect two regions of high velocity dispersion, especially in [ O iii ] maps: in the south close to the peak emission, and in the north of the FOV.

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 4388 near M 87, reports its H-alpha and H I tail, detects associated X-ray emission, and fits a Mach cone to the tail.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388's projected location within the Virgo cluster is close to M 87 ( 1.3^ =385 kpc). Yoshida et al. ( ) discovered an H plume which extends up to 35 kpc northeast from the galaxy center. Based on a Effelsberg 100m telescope H i detection of the H plume, Vollmer Huchtmeier (2003) made dynamical models and concluded that ram pressure stripping can account for the observed extraplanar H and H i emission. Oosterloo van Gorkom (2005) observed NGC 4388 with the WSRT and discovered an H i tail which extends more than 100 kpc to the north. We clearly detect X-ray emission associated to the H i and H tail of NGC 4388.

As for NGC 4569 we adjusted a Mach cone to the X-ray and H i emission distribution with the direction being based on the dynamical model (Vollmer Huchtmeier 2003, Vollmer 2009). The apparent Mach cone angle is 26^ (Fig. fig:n4388m ).

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4388 as a high-velocity Virgo Seyfert whose ram-pressure-stripped HI plume hosts ionized gas, young stars, and in-situ molecular clouds far from the galaxy.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 is moving at a relative velocity redshifted by 1500km/s with respect to M87, and more than 2800 km/s with respect to the M86 group. This strong velocity may explain the violent RPS, the high HI deficiency of NGC 4388 cayatte1990 and the large ( 35kpc) emission-line region found by , northeast of the galaxy. The ionized gas has a mass of 10 ^5 M _ , and is partly excited by the ionizing radiation of the Seyfert 2 nucleus in NGC 4388. The RPS plume is even more extended in HI oosterloo2005 , up to 110 kpc, with a mass of 3.4 x 10 ^8 M _ .

From our molecular cloud and star formation study in the tail north of NGC 4388 in Virgo, we can draw the following conclusions: CO(1--0) and CO(2--1) observations were done with the IRAM 30-m telescope in a total of 11 targets all along the ram-pressure stripped tail northeast NGC4388 in the Virgo Cluster, in order to probe the presence of molecular gas under extreme conditions. Such targets were selected for having strong peaks of HI and H emission. Four of such positions showed CO detections, and 3 of them concentrated in the HaR-2 region, at a distance of 70 kpc of NGC4388, where molecular gas in unexpected. Given the large distances of these sources to NGC4388, it is not likely that the molecular gas was stripped from the galaxy, and must have formed in situ from the HI gas plume.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 4388 as a Virgo spiral stripped by ram pressure, deriving a recent peak event, a wind-disk angle, and gas-resettling origins for disk asymmetries.

quotes.quote: The spiral galaxy NGC 4388 underwent a ram pressure stripping event 200 Myr ago caused by its highly eccentric orbit within the Virgo cluster. This galaxy fulfills all diagnostic criteria for having undergone active ram pressure stripping in the recent past: a strongly truncated H i and H disk, an asymmetric ridge of polarized radio continuum emission, extended extraplanar gas toward the opposite side of the ridge of polarized radio continuum emission, and a recent (a few 100 Myr) quenching of the star formation activity in the outer, gas-free galactic disk. We made dynamical simulations of the ram pressure stripping event to investigate the influence of galactic structure on the observed properties of NGC 4388. The combination of a deep optical spectrum of the outer gas-free region of the galactic disk together with deep H i , H , FUV, and polarized radio continuum data permits to constrain numerical simulations to derive the temporal ram pressure profile, the 3D velocity vector of the galaxy, and the time since peak ram pressure with a high level of confidence.

From the simulations an angle between the ram pressure wind and the galactic disk of 30^ is derived. The galaxy underwent peak ram pressure 240 Myr ago. The observed asymmetries in the disk of NGC 4388 are not caused by the present action of ram pressure, but by the resettling of gas that has been pushed out of the galactic disk during the ram pressure stripping event.

sources.summary: The paper fits NGC 4388 as a nearby megamaser system with only five observed maser features, setting the inclination and using peculiar redshift rather than Hubble-flow recession.

quotes.quote: In this work we perform a Bayesian statistical fit to estimate the mass-to-distance ratio and the recessional redshift of 10 different black holes hosted at the centre of active galactic nuclei, namely the galaxies NGC 5765b, NGC 6323, UGC 3789, CGCG 074-064, ESO 558-G009, NGC 2960, NGC 6264, NGC 4388, J0437+2456 and NGC 2273. Our general relativistic method makes use of the positions in the sky and frequency shift observations of water megamasers circularly orbiting the central black hole on their accretion disks.

NGC 4388 For this megamaser system only five maser features were observed: two redshifted and three blueshifted. Due to the lack of systemic maser observations, the authors MCPIII do not estimate the inclination angle; therefore, we set _0 = 90 and perform the statistical fit for three parameters. Additionally, (according to MCPIII ) this galaxy is located at 19 Mpc, meaning that the galaxy is not into the Hubble flow. For this reason, we use Z_ boost instead of the Z_ rec composition in Eq. ( eq:ztot ) and fit for the peculiar redshift Z_ pec . The best scattering angle we found was = 9 and a reduced ^2 = 1.369 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes decade-long K-band variability in NGC 4388, finding declining warm dust, coronal-line, and Br emission that constrain the AGN torus and nuclear emission regions.

quotes.quote: Here, we have analysed the K-band spectral variability of the Seyfert 1.9/2 galaxy NGC 4388 spanning five epochs over a period of ten years. We have performed spectral synthesis of the nuclear region and found that the contribution of warm dust (T 800 K) declined by 88 during these 10 years. In the same period, the [ Ca VIII ] coronal line decreased 61 , whereas Br emission declined 35 . For the He I and H _2 , we did not detect any significant variation beyond their uncertainties.

NGC 4388 is an SA(s)b galaxy in the constellation of Virgo Veilleux+99 , at a distance of 19 Mpc Kuo+11 . It is one of the brightest galaxies of the Virgo Cluster due to its Seyfert 1.9/2 nucleus Forster+99 . Also, it is nearly edge-on, with an inclination of 79 ^ Damas-Segovia+16 .

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 4388 as a nearby obscured Seyfert-2 galaxy, measuring X-ray reflection variability, Fe K reverberation, and constraints on its reflector geometry.

quotes.quote: We report on a study of the narrow Fe K line and reflection spectrum in the well-known Seyfert-2 AGN, NGC 4388. X-ray spectra summed from two extensive NICER monitoring campaigns, separated by years, show strong evidence of variation in the direct continuum and reflected emission, but only small variations in the obscuring gas. Fits to the spectra from individual NICER observations find a strong, positive correlation between the power-law photon index, , and direct flux that is commonly observed in unobscured AGN.

NGC 4388 is a heavily obscured Seyfert-2 galaxy. At a distance of d = 18.0 Mpc ( sorce2014 ), it is one of the closest examples of this kind of active galactic nucleus (AGN), and one of the brightest in X-rays. Non-thermal X-ray emission from the nucleus has been detected out to nearly 500 keV ( beckmann2004 ). The obscuration that hides the central engine is not Compton-thick, but is only a factor of a few below this key threshold (see, e.g., miller2019 ).

sources.summary: NGC 4388 is described as a nearly edge-on Virgo galaxy with two HI hot spots, closed polarized ridges interpreted as draping sheaths, and a possible Seyfert-powered northern jet.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 Small, red galaxy at 3 o'clock, nearly edge-on orientation ( = 2538 km/s ). There is no clear tail of stripped gas visible in HI which suggests a large LOS component of the velocity which is also consistent with the large red-shift. This galaxy shows two HI hot spots of nearly equal strength, one towards the central-eastern part, the other towards the central-western part of the disk ( _ e =12:25:48 , _ e =12:39:44 , _ w =12:25:45 , _ w =12:39:45 ).

Each of these spots seem to show its own draping sheath around it which appears as a closed ridge of polarised emission with a B-field that is mostly aligned with the disk, but there are perpendicular components above and below the central regions of the disk visible. The vertical B-vectors in the north are most likely associated with a central nuclear outflow that is visible in H . It has been suggested that NGC 4388 hosts a Seyfert 2 nucleus that powers a 15'' jet to the north of the galactic disk .

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4388 as a nearby Virgo spiral with weak evidence for stripped X-ray gas and a known HI tail whose velocity matches the galaxy.

quotes.quote: Two other galaxies are detected in the X-rays nearby: NGC 4388 and NGC 4438. These spiral galaxies have absolute redshifted velocities of 2566 ^ 1 NGC 4388, Rines2008 and 71 ^ 1 NGC 4438, Kenney1995 , respectively.
There is also tentative evidence for diffuse X-ray emission trailing to the east of NGC 4388.

Although the evidence for stripped X-ray emitting gas near NGC 4388 is weak, radio observations of this galaxy show a tail of H i gas trailing to the north of this galaxy, with a velocity structure consistent with NGC 4388 e.g. Oosterloo2005 . All four of these galaxies are therefore undergoing some form of ram pressure stripping, although each galaxy has its own unique observational signatures associated with it. The stripped X-ray emitting gas trailing to the northwest of M86 and the H i tail trailing to the north of NGC 4388 have been detected and discussed in previous work e.g. Randall2008,Oosterloo2005 .

sources.summary: The paper adopts a megamaser black hole mass for NGC 4388, compares its literature CaT dispersions, and uses the CO estimate as a surrogate CaT value.

quotes.quote: Furthermore, we adopted M_ BH estimates of NGC 4388 and NGC 5728 obtained by Greene2016 and Braatz , respectively. Finally, the M_ BH of NGC 5128 is adopted from Cap2009 , in which the authors used stellar kinematics to obtain M_ BH value.

There is no available X-SHOOTER observation for NGC 4388, but there are three available _ CaT estimates from the literature. The reported _ CaT values differ significantly: _ CaT = 119 km s ^ 1 Terlevich , _ CaT = 165 21 km s ^ 1 Riffel and _ CaT = 76 km s ^ 1 Greene2010 . But, the central velocity dispersion measurements of this galaxy, as reported by Greene2010,Saglia,Vanden2016 , are in the range of 100-120 km s ^ 1 , which are consistent with our _ CO (2-0) estimate. Therefore, we used our _ CO (2-0) estimate as a surrogate for _ CaT for NGC 4388.

sources.summary: The paper recovers NGC 4388 in deeper VLBA observations with a slightly elongated core and a southwest feature, and its X-ray spectrum supports a Seyfert 2 disk-maser AGN with a severe FP mass underestimate.

quotes.quote: For the additional snapshot observation sample, the nuclei for both the detections (NGC 3147 and NGC 3516) appear unresolved, but with follow up higher sensitivity deep observations, the core for NGC 2782 and NGC 4388 appear slightly elongated.

In the C-band VLA image of NGC 4388, a resolved additional component separated by 200 pc from central core is seen towards the south-west direction. Our VLBA image also shows a similar elongated feature towards the same direction, but closer to the central peak ( 1 pc).

NGC 4388 (obsid: 60061228002): The \ spectrum of this Seyfert 2 galaxy, one of the few with a disk maser measurement, was fitted with a scattered component (with a fraction of 17 ) added to our baseline model. The \ value derived from the X-ray scaling method (4.3 1.6) 10^6 M_ is in agreement with the dynamical value derived from maser measurements kuo_2011ApJ...727...20K , as explicitly shown in gliozzi_2021MNRAS.502.3329G . The estimate based on the Fundamental Plane for Black Hole Activity ( 2.1 10^5 M_ ) one more time severely underestimates the .

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4388 as an obscured Seyfert galaxy observed with Chandra, applying a moving-fit spectral analysis to track rapid X-ray continuum, absorption, and Fe K variability.

quotes.quote: In the present work we have used this package for investigations of the spectral variability of obscured Seyfert type galaxy NGC 4388. Observations (dataset 2983) were obtained on March 6, 2002 with Chandra ACIS detector in the LETG mode. Light curve for this AGN one can see in the Fig.6.

First of all the curve for power law normalization parameter is practically identical to the light curve (Fig.6). There is also some possibility for smooth changes of the hydrogen column with time-scale about 40 ksec. The curve for the Fe K line central intensity shows flare during which _ K and E_ K were probably suppressed.

sources.summary: NGC 4388 is described as an inclined Sy2 disk galaxy where stellar contamination affects the near-IR SED, while the torus model fits some bands but underestimates Q-band flux.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388. This highly inclined disk galaxy is spectroscopically classified as a Sy2 nucleus Phillips82 . However, Shields96 report the detection of weak, broad H emission. The ground-based L-band point introduces a bump in the SED around 3-4 , which likely represents starlight contamination ( stellar_contamination ). The galaxy appear very extended in the L band Alonso03 , making difficult to isolate of the nuclear emission.

sources.summary: The paper links young intracluster star-forming regions to NGC 4388's ram-pressure-stripped tail, arguing that their velocities and ages show star formation after gas left the galaxy.

quotes.quote: Two of them were at a projected distance of 35 kpc away from NGC 4388, and the other two were 66 kpc away. Our new spectroscopic observation revealed that their recession velocities were comparable to the ram-pressure-stripped tail of NGC 4388 and confirmed their association. The stellar mass of the star-forming regions ranged from 10^ 4 10^ 4.5 M_ except for that of the faintest one which would be <10^3 M _ . The metallicity was comparable to the solar abundance, and the age of the stars was 10^ 6.8 years. Their young stellar age meant that the star formation should have started after the gas was stripped from NGC 4388.

sources.summary: The paper reports a two-maximum or centrally depressed [ FeII ] profile in NGC 4388, co-rotating disk gas, and a flat velocity-dispersion profile across its central kiloparsec.

quotes.quote: The H _2 line-to-continuum ratio is constant or drops significantly towards the nucleus in most galaxies, except NGC 2273, NGC 5273, and NGC 7450. For Br , in contrast, the ratio peaks towards the nucleus in many galaxies, consistent with stronger ionization mechanisms towards the active nucleus. These two results both explain the observed central drop of the H _2 /Br ratio. Of the three emission lines, the [ FeII ] line-to-continuum ratio shows the most distinct radial variations. Its profile is centrally peaked in Mrk 1, Mrk 3, NGC 262, NGC 1320, NGC 2273, NGC 3081, NGC 5273, and NGC 7450. Several sources show two maxima (or a central drop, e.g. NGC 1667, NGC 3185, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, NGC 4388, and NGC 5506). Finally, Mrk 1066, NGC 931, NGC 2110, NGC 2992, and NGC 4051 show peculiar distributions, e.g. the distribution in NGC 2110 shows three separate peaks.

However, the gas velocity curves are similar to those of the stars, which define the galaxy disk. This is the case in Mrk 1, Mrk 1066, NGC 262, NGC 931, NGC 1667, NGC 2110, NGC 2273, NGC 3081, NGC 3185, NGC 4388, NGC 5273, and NGC 5506. In these galaxies, all three emission lines display a velocity curve that follows that of the stars and is reminiscent of rotation, thus strongly implying that the line-emitting gas is located in the disk of the galaxy and rotating with the disk. Other galaxies (Mrk 3, NGC 1320, NGC 2992, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, and NGC 7450) display velocity curves different from the stellar velocity curve in one or more emission lines, which may be a signature of non-rotational motion, either in- or outflows or other non-planar motion of the gas.

The radial dispersion profiles in Mrk 3, Mrk 1066, NGC 2110, NGC 2273, NGC 4051, NGC 4151, NGC 4253, NGC 5273, and NGC 5506 have a central peak, which indicates a higher kinetic gas temperature in the nucleus. Either the ISM is more turbulent (possibly caused by a jet- or wind-driven outflow), and/or more intensely heated by the incident radiation field. In either case, a peaked dispersion distribution is a strong indication for the AGN governing the physical state of the gas at the nucleus. The dispersion peak is most often present in [ FeII ] (indicating dust destruction close to the nucleus), and/or in Br (indicating a strong radiation field), while the H _2 line shows the least-pronounced central rise. Examples for flat velocity dispersion profiles are NGC 3081, NGC 3185, and NGC 4388, which show no significant change over up to 1.2 kpc in diameter. The velocity dispersion in all three sources is of order 100 km/s, well within the range observed for star forming regions, which is consistent with star formation being the dominant the gas excitation mechanism. Finally, the dispersion profiles in Mrk 1, NGC 262, NGC 931, NGC 1320, NGC 1667, NGC 2992, and NGC 7450 show marked differences between the three emission lines, with variations of 100 km/s or more at a given radius. This indicates that the ionized atomic and molecular gas is responding differently to the central AGN in these sources, perhaps due to the presences of asymmetric energy input (a jet or shocks) together with a stratified medium, e.g. a thin molecular disk in a thick ionized medium.

sources.summary: The paper obtains a reasonable Swift fit for NGC 4388 but finds high absorption and a very low photon index make its intrinsic luminosity unreliable.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 Sufficient Swift data were available to produce 4 flux-binned spectra of NGC 4388. We also obtained a reasonable fit to the Swift data with a power law and a neutral absorber ( ^2_R = 1.16 ). This model did not require the photon index (or absorption) to vary between flux-binned spectra, though the spectra only cover a small range of fluxes and therefore may not be expected to change significantly. Previous studies have also fitted the spectrum with a model consisting of as absorbed power law e.g. Elvis2004,Risaliti2002 . However, we obtain a very low value for the photon index ( = 1.10 ), which does not agree with the value found in previous studies - together with the high level of absorption ( N_H 3 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ), which is great enough to affect the majority of the Swift XRT energy range, were therefore conclude that the underlying spectrum, and therefore the intrinsic luminosity, of NGC 4388 are not well constrained.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 4388 among usable higher-order HEG AGN data, treats it as type 2, and finds its higher-order Fe-line width smaller than first-order results.

quotes.quote: We searched Transmission Grating Data Archive and Catalog TGCat, TG , and found that besides Circinus, Mrk 3, and NGC 1068, the second and third order HEG data of NGC 3783, NGC 4151, NGC 4388, and NGC 4151 are also usable. Among them, NGC 3783 and NGC 4151 are classified as type 1.5 AGN, while the others are type 2 AGN. All the spectra are extracted from a region with a 2 arcsec half-width in the cross-dispersion direction.

While for Mrk 3, NGC 1068, NGC 4388, and NGC 4507, the measured line widths are also smaller than those measured from the first order HEG spectra. The results clearly show that the line widths of the Fe line measured from the first order HEG spectra are over-estimated.

sources.summary: The paper finds low-surface-brightness plumes and diffuse light around NGC 4388, linking its ionized and HI tails to ram pressure plus tidal stripping of young disk stars.

quotes.quote: The other edge-on spiral galaxy in the field, NGC 4388 (2513 ), also shows low surface brightness features extending from both sides of its major axis. The system shows ionized emission extending 7 \ to the northeast (Yoshida 2002), as well as an even more extended HI tail stretching nearly 30 \ along the same direction, likely due to ram pressure stripping in the cluster environment (Oosterloo van Gorkom 2005). At the base of the HI tail, near the region of ionized emission, we also detect diffuse light at =27.5 with a broadband color ( =0.66), somewhat bluer than the tidal features we see elsewhere in Virgo. On the other side of the disk, the western plume is even bluer, with colors ranging from =0.5--0.6, suggestive of young stars being stripped from the galaxy's disk, or perhaps collisionally-induced star formation in the tidal feature. Given the intracluster HII region projected 3.5 \ (17 kpc) to the north of the galaxy (Gerhard 2002) as well, the complex structure around NGC 4388 is a good example of how tidal stripping and ram pressure stripping can work in tandem to deposit young stars into the intracluster environment.

sources.summary: The paper reports NGC 4388 has centrally peaked velocity dispersion and a weaker h3-velocity anti-correlation trend.

quotes.quote: Patches of low- are seen for Mrk 607, NGC 2110, NGC 3516, NGC 4051, NGC 4235 and NGC 5899, while three objects (NGC 1052, NGC 3227 and NGC 4388) show a centrally peaked distribution. These rings and partial rings are located at distances from the nucleus ranging between 150 and 250 pc. Nuclear values for each galaxy are presented in the last column of Table tab_mod , obtained by fitting the nuclear spectrum integrated within a circular aperture with radius of 75 pc. Most galaxies show low h_ 3 values ( 0.10 <h_ 3 < 0.10), suggesting that their LOSVDs are well represented by a Gaussian velocity distribution. Exceptions are Mrk 607, Mrk1066 and NGC 4051 that show h_ 3 values larger than 0.15. For 8 galaxies (NGC 1052, NGC 2110, NGC 3227, NGC 3516, NGC 4051, NGC 5506, Mrk 607 and Mrk 1066), the h_ 3 map is anti-correlated with the velocity field, with positive h_ 3 values seen at locations where rotation velocities are observed in blueshifts and negative h_ 3 values associated to regions where the rotation velocities are observed in redshifts.
A similar trend (but not so clear) is observed for other four galaxies (NGC 4235, NGC 4388, NGC 5929 and Mrk 1157).

sources.summary: The paper models four hard-X-ray observations of NGC 4388 above 3 keV, finding Compton-thin absorption and evidence for absorption and emission variability.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388: The source has been observed three times with \ and one time with . We fit the spectra from the four observations above 3 keV in order to avoid the complexity of modeling the soft X-rays in this source see e.g., Miller19 . We considered total obscuration by fixing C_ sc to zero in order to avoid overestimating the soft emission. We kept tied between the four observations and let the power law normalization and N_ H vary. For MYTC the fit requires A 1 .

sources.summary: The paper emphasizes NGC 4388 has only five megamaser spots, inflated mass uncertainty, a Compton-thin X-ray model, and sensitivity to photon-index assumptions.

quotes.quote: For the uncertainties on the \ determined via megamaser measurements we used the errors quoted in the literature with the exception of NGC 4388, for which we multiplied the quoted uncertainty by a factor of 10; this yields a percentage error of 24 , which better reflects the actual uncertainty on the \ in this source, where there is no systemic maser detected and the five maser spots detected are not sufficient to demonstrate that the rotation is Keplerian kuo11 .

NGC 4388, located in the Virgo cluster, has only five megamaser spots, which make it impossible to demonstrate that they lie on a disk or that the rotation is Keplerian kuo11 . For this reason, to reflect the actual uncertainty on the \ derived by megamaser measurements, we have increased the statistical error by a factor of 10, leading to an uncertainty of 24 .

NGC 4388: The starting spectral model for this source is once more provided by the work of mas16 , who fitted the \ spectrum with the \ and \ models, which favor a Compton-thin scenario with a substantial scattered primary emission that dominates below 5 keV. The results from our baseline model are fully consistent with their results.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 4388 as one of three Virgo edge-on LTGs with significant extraplanar X-ray emission, noting prior studies and evidence related to ram-pressure stripping.

quotes.quote: Somewhat surprisingly, only three galaxies (NGC 4192, NGC 4216 and NGC 4388) are found to have S/N > 3 , indicating significant extraplanar X-ray emission. To our knowledge, this is the first report of significant extended X-ray emission from NGC 4192 and NGC 4216, although the S/N is only moderate ( 4 ). Diffuse X-ray emission in and around NGC 4388 has been extensively studied by ROSAT Matt1994,Colbert1998 , Chandra Iwasawa2003,Yi2021 and XMM-Newon Wezgowie2011 observations. Unfortunately, the limited S/N of these three galaxies does not permit a meaningful X-ray spectral analysis for the extraplanar hot gas, for which we also adopt the above photon flux-to-luminosity conversion.
The only three galaxies (NGC 4192, NGC 4216 and NGC 4388) with significant extraplanar X-ray emission also have its 0.5--2 keV luminosity within this range.
In fact, at least in the case of NGC 4388, evidence was found for extended X-ray emission related to RPS Wezgowie2011 .

sources.summary: The paper presents sub-parsec VLBA imaging and radio SED results for NGC 4388, describing its central radio morphology and spectral shape in the FRAMEx AGN sample.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 [ 12 ^ h 25 ^ m 46 78135, + 12 39 43 761 ] : All four band images revealed a weak central radio emission. NGC 4388 is the weakest of all radio sources detected in the L, C, and X bands. No K-band emission was detected. The four-frequency SED plot with the K band upper limit shows an inverted spectrum with a spectral index of 0.3. NGC 4593 [ 12 ^ h 39 ^ m 39 44359, 05 20 39 035 ] : Similar to NGC 4388, images reveal a weak compact core-like emission at the center.

sources.summary: NGC 4388 is described as a Virgo Seyfert undergoing ram-pressure effects, with nuclear activity and a radio source suggesting a collimated AGN-driven outflow plus extended optical line emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 was the first Seyfert galaxy discovered in the Virgo cluster (Phillips Malin 1982). It is plunging edge-on at 1500 km s ^ 1 through the center of the Virgo cluster and experiencing the effects of ram-pressure stripping by the densest portions of the intracluster medium (ICM; e.g., Cayatte et al. 1994). Nuclear activity has been detected at nearly all wavelengths. The morphology of the nuclear radio source suggests a collimated AGN-driven outflow (Stone et al. 1988; Falcke, Wilson, Simpson 1998). At optical wavelengths, NGC 4388 has been known for some time to present extended line emission (e.g., Pogge 1988; Corbin, Baldwin, Wilson 1988 and references therein).

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4388 as an edge-on Seyfert barred spiral whose active nucleus may dominate, with far-infrared diagnostics suggesting mixed stellar and non-stellar excitation.

quotes.quote: On optical images, VCC 836 = NGC 4388 unambiguously resembles classical early-type spirals, with a prominent bulge crossed by thick dust lanes; VCC 460 = NGC 4293 stands between H I deficient and H I normal galaxies, and also has an early-type aspect.

For NGC 4388, we cannot conclude and the active nucleus may be dominant. We can only mention that its global color is lower than that of VCC 1326 = NGC 4491, and this is marginally true as well for the nucleus, and that the nucleus of VCC 1326 is not classified as active Far-infrared diagnostics of nuclear activity are ambiguous for NGC 4388: its infrared to radio flux ratio as defined by Condon is q = 2.27 , and its spectral index between 25 and 60 m Grijp is = 1.23 . Both values indicate that stellar and non-stellar excitations may contribute in comparable amounts to the infrared energy output.

NGC 4519 and IC 1953 are similar SBd galaxies, VCC 1326 = NGC 4491 is a small and low-luminosity SBa, and VCC 836 = NGC 4388 is an edge-on Seyfert SBab (for which the molecular content may be ill-determined due to the integration of the CO line throughout the disk).

sources.summary: The paper presents Chandra observations of Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC4388, finding an obscured hard X-ray nucleus, extended soft emission aligned with optical ionization structures, and low-ionization photoionized gas likely powering the nebula.

quotes.quote: We report on X-ray emission from the Seyfert 2 galaxy NGC4388 observed with the Chandra X-ray Observatory. A hard X-ray peak is found at the position of the active nucleus suggested by optical and radio observations. Extended soft X-ray emission correlates well with the ionization cone found in optical line emission. A large soft X-ray extension is found up to 16 kpc (and possibly 30 kpc) to the north of the galaxy. Photoionized gas with low ionization parameters (log 0.4) appears to be the likely explanation of this emission.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as a Seyfert 2 with jet and nuclear-outflow radio polarization, truncated disk and H I emission, asymmetric extensions, and no disk-wide radio halo.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 harbors a Seyfert 2 nucleus with an associated bright radio source and a 15'' long jet to the north (Hummel Saikia 1991). The disk and jet emission perpendicular to the galactic plane has already been detected at by Hummel et al. (1983). In Fig.  fig:n4388 we observe emission from the nuclear outflow in polarized 6 cm emission with vertical magnetic field vectors north of the nucleus. The radio continuum disk emission at 20 cm and 6 cm and the H i emission distribution are truncated at about half the optical radius.

sources.summary: The paper characterizes NGC 4388 as a core-region Seyfert spiral that is heavily H i stripped, has a long tail, and is dominated by central radio emission.

quotes.quote: NGC 4388 NGC 4388 is a Seyfert 2 Sb-type spiral galaxy located close to the cluster core at the distance of only 1 3 (390 kpc in the sky plane) from Virgo A. The H i observations of Oosterloo van Gorkom ( ), as well as H observations of Veilleux et al. ( ) and Yoshida et al. ( ) suggest that this galaxy is heavily stripped and highly deficient in H i , forming a 100 kpc long tail, and that the observed H outflow is most likely a partial ionisation of this tail by an active nuclei of the galaxy. The total power emission (Fig. 4388tp ) is dominated by the central region of the galaxy (over 119 mJy at 1.4 GHz) and by two NVSS radio background sources placed symmetrically on both sides of the galaxy center (J 122551+123951 and J 122540+123958 with total flux densities of 4 mJy and 7.9 mJy respectively). The low surface-brightness extension to the west is very likely a mixture of the NVSS radio background source J122528+124111 with a total flux density of 2.5 mJy at 1.4 GHz and FIRST radio background source J122519.2+123854 with a total flux density of 13.05 mJy at 1.4 GHz.

sources.summary: NGC 4388 is used as a bright obscured Compton-thin AGN example where simulated ASTRO-H spectra resolve iron-line structure and constrain torus ionization, radius, and bulk motion.

quotes.quote: Figure 4 shows a simulated 300 ks SXS spectra in the iron-K band for NGC 4388, the brightest obscured Compton-thin AGN with a 14--195 keV flux of F=2.8 10^ 10 erg s^ 1 cm^ 2 baumgartner12 . It is clearly seen that details of the 6.4 keV line profile are resolved, in this case revealing the double-peak structure characteristic of the (assumed) keplerian disk. We can use the centroid and K /K line ratios to constrain the dominant ionization state of the iron; for the near torus case, we obtain the range Fe II--Fe VI, consistent with the XSTAR model that is dominated by Fe II. In this simulated case, we can also constrain the inner radius of the torus to better than 3 , and measure mean bulk motion with a precision of < 100 km s ^ 1 . While the parameters of this simulation are clearly simplistic, it highlights the fact that ASTRO-H will open a new window on AGN tori.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 4388 was strongly affected by M87 and M84 contamination; peeling and self-calibration improved the data, but L-band rms remained too high for polarized-flux detection.

quotes.quote: Three Virgo galaxies: NGC 4192, NGC 4388 and NGC 4438 These galaxies are highly affected by contamination from strong sources in the field, such as M87, rendering higher than normal rms noise and artifacts hard to clean out. This may have an effect on both spectral index results and polarization.

NGC 4388 was particularly affected by M87, and we made an attempt at peeling the source in L-band, as well as self calibrating on M84 (the other disturbing source in the field), which rendered some improvement. The resulting rms at L-band was too high for us to detect significant polarized flux (panel f).

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as a Virgo galaxy with a wide HI tail, a well-truncated highly deficient HI disk, and model-dependent ram-pressure requirements.

quotes.quote: The HI tail left by NGC 4388 oosterloo05 has a width between 15 and 40 kpc.
Objects whose HI maps (from VIVA) show a well-truncated HI disk without any extending plume are found to be highly deficient. NGC 4388 and NGC 4569 also belong to this class.
NGC 4388 requires 3300 (or 2600) instead of 5000 vollmer03 , but damas16 report that a value three times lower is needed, i.e. 1700 .

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 4388 as an inclined Seyfert 2 galaxy with extended soft X-ray and ionized gas structures and a radio double source offset from the galaxy center.
